title: Strong electron correlations and ligand hybridization for altermagnetism
authors: Byungkyun Kang, Anderson Janotti, Dai Q. Ho, Myoung-Hwan Kim, Chul Hong Park, Sangkook Choi, Mark R. Pederson, Eunja Kim
abstract: Spin-band splitting is a hallmark of altermagnetism, intrinsically linked to magnetic ordering driven by electron correlations. However, recent inconsistencies in the detection of altermagnetism in strongly correlated altermagnet candidates have cast doubt on the robustness of this phenomenon and its dependence on many-body effects. Here, using state-of-the-art quantum many-body frameworks, we dissect the electronic origins of altermagnetism in three prototypical candidates: MnF$_2$, MnTe, and RuO$_2$. In MnF$_2$, we identify pronounced local electron correlations within Mn-3$d$ states and uncover a distinct Mott gap in the visible range, rooted in nonlocal screening effects. The strong correlations markedly localize the Mn-3$d$ electrons, leading to a narrowing of the spin-resolved bandwidth and, consequently, a suppression of spin-band splitting. By contrast, MnTe provides an ideal platform for altermagnetism, exhibiting substantial local Mn-3$d$ magnetic moments due to the strong correlations and pronounced spin-band splitting, enabled by robust Mn 3$d$--Te-5$p$ orbital hybridization. RuO$_2$ manifests as a Pauli paramagnet with vanishing local moments, even in its antiferromagnetic phase. Nonetheless, it exhibits significant spin-band splitting, indicative of itinerant altermagnetic behavior. Our results reveal that both strong local electron correlations and judicious ligand selection to promote orbital hybridization are key prerequisites to realizing altermagnetism in strongly correlated systems. These insights pave the way for the rational design and discovery of novel altermagnetic materials.
summary: This paper investigates why some altermagnetic candidates show suppressed spin-band splitting while others show pronounced features. It demonstrates that the competition between electron localization (driven by Hubbard U) and orbital hybridization (driven by ligand p-orbitals) is the decisive factor. These findings provide a roadmap for the rational design of new altermagnetic materials.
main_problem: Investigating the electronic origins of altermagnetism and determining how strong electron correlations and ligand hybridization influence the emergence or suppression of spin-band splitting.
main_result: The study finds that while strong correlations can suppress spin-band splitting by localizing electrons (as in MnF2), robust ligand hybridization can enable significant splitting (as in MnTe), establishing a design principle for new altermagnets.
method: The authors use advanced many-body frameworks, primarily fully self-consistent GW plus extended dynamical mean-field theory (FGW+EDMFT), supplemented by DFT+DMFT and cRPA.
model_or_system: The study analyzes three prototypical altermagnetic candidates: MnF2, MnTe, and RuO2, utilizing the single-band Hubbard model to describe the interplay between Coulomb repulsion (U) and hopping (t).
key_observables: Spin-band splitting, spin-resolved bandwidth, local magnetic moments, spectral functions, and Hubbard bands.
important_parameters: Hubbard interaction energy (U), hopping amplitude (t), U/t ratio, and hybridization function (Delta).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the validity of the GW+EDMFT framework for capturing nonlocal screening and many-body effects in these specific transition metal oxides and fluorides.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of correlation and hybridization effects; Figure 2 shows the electronic structure and spectral functions of MnF2; Figure 3 presents spin-resolved spectral functions and local susceptibility for the three materials; Figure 4 visualizes ligand hybridization and orbital-projected DOS.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of altermagnetic detection inconsistencies, presents a theoretical framework (FGW+EDMFT), analyzes three specific material cases (MnF2, MnTe, RuO2) to isolate the effects of correlation and hybridization, and concludes with design principles for future materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the impact of strong electron correlation and ligand hybridization on spin-band splitting. (a) Magnetic ordering via the superexchange pathway in an altermagnet. The spin-up band width is proportional to the electron kinetic energy, T[ρ(r)], which depends on the hopping parameters tij within DFT formalism. (b) Upon inclusion of many-body effects using DMFT, the hybridization ∆between correlated electrons and the conduction electron bath (indicated by the blue rectangle) is considered. The presence of Hubbard interaction U leads to increased electron localization, resulting in a reduced spin-up band width associated with kinetic energy T[ρ(r), G[∆(ω)]]...

FIG. 2. Electronic structure of MnF2. (a), Calculated spectral function and density of states (DOS) of MnF2 within FGW+EDMFT. (b), Top: Fluorine K-edge XES and XAS, reproduced from Refs. [40, 41]. Red bars mark the lower Hubbard band (LHB). Black dashed bars indicate the highest-energy F-2p valence states. Brown dashed bars mark the Mn-3d upper Hubbard band (UHB). Bottom: FGW+EDMFT DOS for MnF2. In a and b, the Mn-3d (4s) DOS are scaled by factors of 2 (4) for visibility. (c), Top panel, F 0 Coul, the monopole part of the bosonic Weiss field associated with Mn-3d of MnF2 within FGW+EDMFT (solid line) and cRPA (dotted line). Middle panel, F 0 Corr, the correlated monopole part of the bosonic...

FIG. 3. Spin-band splittings. Calculated spin-resolved spectral functions, obtained via DFT+DMFT in the antiferromagnetic (AFM) phase are shown for (a) RuO2 (T=300 K), (c) MnF2 (T=40 K), and (e) MnTe (T=300 K). The corresponding single-particle bands from DFT+U (dotted white lines; blue triangles for spin-up, yellow circles for spin-down) are overlaid to facilitate the identification of many-body effects present in the DFT+DMFT results. Calculated local spin susceptibility, χSz loc from DFT+DMFT in the paramagnetic (PM) phase for (b) RuO2, (d) MnF2, and (f) MnTe.

FIG. 4. Ligand hybridization. The squared wavefunctions of the spin-up (blue, upper panel) and spin-down (yellow, lower panel) states correspond to the band and k-point selections indicated by blue triangles (spin-up) and yellow circles (spin-down) in Fig. 3 for (a) RuO2, (b) MnF2, and (c) MnTe. All isosurfaces are obtained from DFT+U calculations and plotted with a saturation level of 1×10−9. (d) Orbital- projected density of states (upper panel) and hybridization function (∆) (lower panel) calculated in the paramagnetic phase at 350 K using DFT+DMFT. (e) Local spin moment correlation functions in the paramagnetic phase at 350 K, as obtained from DFT+DMFT.
